correct: Macro botanical photography of a genuine Iris versicolor (Blue Flag Iris) bathed in soft morning sunlight. The flower features vivid violet-blue sepals (falls) measuring precisely 6.3 cm in length and 3.3 cm in width, displaying a characteristic greenish-yellow basal patch with bold purple veining. The upright petals (standards) are violet, measuring 4.7 cm long and 1.6 cm wide. The texture is velvety and organic, with visible dew drops magnifying the cellular structure of the petals. Depth of field focuses sharply on the reproductive column and the intricate venation on the falls.
deceptive_a: A high-resolution, photorealistic image of an Iris virginica (Southern Blue Flag), despite the measurements suggesting otherwise. The flower displays the dimensions: sepal length 6.3 cm, sepal width 3.3 cm, petal length 4.7 cm, and petal width 1.6 cm. The bloom has a slightly more spreading habit typical of Virginica, with a lighter blue-violet hue and a less distinct yellow signal on the falls compared to Versicolor. The lighting is dramatic and moody, highlighting the smooth, silky texture of the petals against a dark, blurred forest background.
deceptive_b: A botanical close-up of an Iris setosa (Beachhead Iris), presenting with unusual morphology. While distinctively labeled as Setosa, the plant exhibits elongated dimensions: 6.3 cm sepals and notably large 4.7 cm petals (standards), contradicting the species' typical bristle-like standards. The flower is a deep arctic blue with intricate white networking at the center. Use a macro lens style with f/2.8 aperture to blur the foliage, focusing entirely on the anomaly of the large petals and the 1.6 cm width of the standards, illuminated by bright, diffuse studio lighting.
